environment.hardware: No special hardware required. The accompanying code is pure functional numerical Haskell operating on small (2x2 and 3x3) complex matrices; verified on Apple Silicon (aarch64) macOS 25.5.0 (Darwin) with no GPU, cluster, or large-memory dependency.
environment.software: GHC 9.14.1 and Cabal 3.16.1.0 (Hackage index-state 2026-07-14T20:00:04Z) for the Haskell verification suite; pdfTeX 3.141592653-2.6-1.40.29 (TeX Live 2026) for the LaTeX build.
dependencies: base >=4 && <5 (bundled with GHC, no version conflict)
QuickCheck 2.18.0.0 (resolved from Hackage; not vendored, pinned, or declared anywhere in the repository)

concerns: src/positivity-cstar-norms/ ships five bare .hs files (Core.hs, GNS.hs, Main.hs, Proofs.hs, Properties.hs) with no .cabal file, stack.yaml, or README, unlike the sibling lattice-eft-equivalence topic which ships lattice-eft-equivalence.cabal with declared build-depends. Main.hs transitively imports Test.QuickCheck via Properties.hs, and QuickCheck is not present in GHC's global package database, so a naive `ghc Main.hs` or `runghc Main.hs` fails immediately with a missing-module error until the user independently figures out the QuickCheck dependency and constructs a cabal/stack project themselves, as I had to do to run this suite.

The paper's abstract states that 'Accompanying Haskell code verifies positivity, the GNS construction, and the Bloch-ball geometry of qubit states on finite matrix algebras,' but I found no repository URL, DOI, Zenodo archive, or supplementary-material pointer anywhere in the manuscript text or bibliography. A reader of the PDF in isolation has no way to locate the code; it is only discoverable via the shared project repository.

No dependency pin (e.g. a cabal.project.freeze or an explicit QuickCheck upper bound) is recorded, so a build at a different Hackage index-state than the one used here could in principle resolve a different QuickCheck major version. Risk is low in practice: the property definitions use only long-stable QuickCheck API (Arbitrary, Gen, choose, ==>, quickCheckResult).

The Hermitian eigenvalue routine (Core.hs, jacobiEigenvalues/rotateAt) is a hand-rolled cyclic Jacobi solver with fixed numerical tolerances (1e-14 sweep convergence; assorted 1e-6-1e-12 tolerances for positivity/equality checks) rather than a vetted linear-algebra library such as hmatrix or LAPACK bindings. This is a reasonable and transparently documented choice for the paper's stated goal of a dependency-light demonstration on small fixed-size matrices, and I observed no numerical instability at the tested sizes, but the approach is not established to scale beyond the 2x2/3x3 cases the paper actually exercises.
